policy_statement: Develop tools/resources to help municipalities procure energy and enable direct wholesale market energy purchases.
mechanism_description: Developing tools and resources helps municipalities purchase electricity directly from the wholesale market, enabling cleaner electricity procurement that can reduce power-sector emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Electricity procurement decisions → ↑ renewable/low-carbon generation share → ↓ fossil generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in activity/system: Municipal electricity purchasing practices shift (e.g., through direct wholesale market access, aggregation, contracting support) toward sourcing renewable or lower-emissions electricity. (2) Mediating process: Procurement choices alter generation dispatch and/or investment signals (e.g., via PPAs/market participation/REC-backed contracts), increasing the share of renewables and decreasing reliance on fossil-fired generation. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced CO₂e emissions from electricity consumption across municipal operations and potentially the wider community served, lowering overall power-sector emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing power-sector GHG emissions by enabling municipalities to procure lower-carbon/renewable electricity instead of fossil-based electricity.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: enabling cleaner electricity procurement that increases renewable/low-carbon supply and displaces fossil generation, leading to lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can improve cost management or administrative efficiency, the principal climate-relevant outcome described is emissions reduction from cleaner electricity (Mitigation), not primarily reduced energy use (Resource Efficiency) or hazard risk reduction (Adaptation) or ecosystem carbon/heat benefits (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: If “direct wholesale purchasing” were used mainly for cheaper fossil electricity, mitigation benefits would not materialize; however, the mechanism as defined explicitly enables cleaner/renewable procurement, so classification follows the intended dominant pathway.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that develops tools/resources to enable municipal direct procurement of cleaner electricity acts through the same procurement-to-generation mix pathway, with mitigation as the dominant category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific subtypes (e.g., bundled RECs vs. new-build PPAs, additionality, scope of load served) require policy-level evidence and are not assumed here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Benefits depend on procurement design and “additionality” (e.g., unbundled RECs vs. new renewable capacity); in some contexts wholesale access may increase exposure to price volatility (not a climate category) or could inadvertently increase fossil sourcing if not constrained—those are implementation-specific and do not change the dominant intended mitigation pathway of this mechanism.
key_indicators: "develop tools and resources"; "help municipalities procure energy"; "enable direct purchases of energy"; "from the wholesale market"; "Support direct energy purchasing"
co_benefits: Potential municipal cost savings/budget predictability; improved energy price risk management; increased local energy market participation/competition
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be coded as an incentive-program if paired with funding/subsidies, or as a regulatory change if it included authorization/mandates for wholesale access; the provided text is limited to tools/resources (enabling support) only.
